questions.question: The beneficiary principle, as established in Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804], provides that a trust must have:

options.A: A charitable purpose recognised by equity
options.B: Identifiable beneficiaries in whose favour the court can decree performance
options.C: A minimum duration of at least one year
options.D: The consent of the Attorney General before it becomes operative

questions.explanation: In Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804], Grant MR stated that every trust must have a definite object and there must be somebody in whose favour the court can decree performance. This is the foundational beneficiary principle: a trust for a purpose with no beneficiary to enforce it is generally void.

questions.question: Which judge and case articulated the statement that 'there must be somebody in whose favour the court can decree performance' in the context of purpose trusts?

options.A: Lord Eldon in Attorney General v Brown [1818]
options.B: Grant MR in Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804]
options.C: Goff J in Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969]
options.D: Lord Wilberforce in McPhail v Doulton [1971]

questions.explanation: Grant MR in Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804] articulated the classic statement of the beneficiary principle: every trust must have a definite object and there must be somebody in whose favour the court can decree performance. This case remains the foundational authority on the invalidity of pure purpose trusts.

questions.question: A testator leaves a sum of money 'for the maintenance of my horses and hounds.' Based on the decision in Re Dean [1889], this trust is:

options.A: Void as a pure purpose trust with no beneficiaries
options.B: Valid as a charitable trust for the benefit of animals
options.C: An anomalous exception upheld as a trust of imperfect obligation
options.D: Valid only if the animals are owned by the Crown

questions.explanation: In Re Dean [1889], a trust for the maintenance of the testator's horses and hounds was upheld for a period of fifty years as an anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle. Such trusts are described as trusts of imperfect obligation — valid but unenforceable because there is no beneficiary with standing to compel the trustee to perform.

questions.question: What is the primary reason anomalous non-charitable purpose trusts are described as 'trusts of imperfect obligation'?

options.A: They cannot be registered with the Charities Regulator
options.B: They are valid but unenforceable because no beneficiary has standing to compel performance
options.C: They impose a moral but not legal obligation on the trustee
options.D: They require approval from the High Court before taking effect

questions.explanation: Trusts of imperfect obligation are valid in the sense that the trustee may carry them out, but unenforceable because there is no beneficiary with standing to compel performance. They rely entirely on the trustee's willingness to act. Examples include trusts for the maintenance of specific animals (Re Dean [1889]) and grave maintenance (Pirbright v Salwey [1896]).

questions.question: In Pirbright v Salwey [1896], which category of anomalous non-charitable purpose trust was upheld?

options.A: A trust for the saying of private masses
options.B: A trust for the maintenance of a pet dog
options.C: A trust for the maintenance of a grave
options.D: A trust for the promotion of political causes

questions.explanation: In Pirbright v Salwey [1896], a trust for the maintenance of a grave was upheld as an anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle. This is one of three recognised categories of anomalous non-charitable purpose trusts: maintenance of specific animals, erection and maintenance of graves and monuments, and saying of private masses.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognised anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle for non-charitable purpose trusts?

options.A: Trusts for the maintenance of specific animals
options.B: Trusts for the erection and maintenance of graves and monuments
options.C: Trusts for the saying of private masses
options.D: Trusts for the promotion of amateur sporting events generally

questions.explanation: The three recognised anomalous exceptions to the beneficiary principle are: trusts for the maintenance of specific animals (Re Dean [1889]), trusts for the erection and maintenance of graves and monuments (Pirbright v Salwey [1896]), and trusts for the saying of private masses. General promotion of sporting events does not fall within these narrow anomalous exceptions.

questions.question: In Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969], Goff J held that a trust for the maintenance of a sports ground for the use of employees was valid. The basis for this decision was that:

options.A: Sport and recreation constitutes a charitable purpose under the Charities Act 2009
options.B: The purpose, though expressed as a purpose, was in substance for the benefit of ascertainable individuals who could enforce it
options.C: The trust was an anomalous exception similar to Re Dean [1889]
options.D: The employees had provided consideration by working for the company

questions.explanation: In Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969], Goff J held that where a purpose trust, though expressed as a purpose, is in substance for the benefit of ascertainable individuals, it can be valid. The employees were identifiable individuals who could enforce the trust. This is not an anomalous exception but a principled extension: if identifiable persons benefit, the beneficiary principle is satisfied.

questions.question: The significance of the Re Denley [1969] principle for FE-1 purposes is best stated as:

options.A: It abolished the beneficiary principle for all purpose trusts
options.B: A trust expressed as a purpose but benefiting identifiable individuals may be valid and enforceable by those individuals
options.C: It extended the list of anomalous exceptions to include employee welfare trusts
options.D: It created a new category of charitable trust for employee benefit

questions.explanation: The Re Denley [1969] principle provides that if a trust is expressed as a purpose but benefits identifiable individuals, those individuals can enforce it and the trust is valid. This is not a charitable trust and not an anomalous exception — it is a purposive trust that is saved because it benefits ascertainable beneficiaries who have standing to enforce it.

questions.question: A testator creates a trust 'for the purpose of maintaining a reading room for the general public.' This trust will most likely be:

options.A: Valid as a charitable trust for the advancement of education
options.B: Void as a non-charitable purpose trust with no identifiable beneficiaries
options.C: Valid under the Re Denley principle as members of the public are ascertainable
options.D: Valid as an anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle

questions.explanation: A trust for the 'general public' does not have identifiable beneficiaries in the sense required by Re Denley [1969] — it is not a class of persons who can enforce the trust as individuals. Unless it qualifies as a charitable trust (which would require satisfying the public benefit test and falling within a recognised charitable head), it will fail as a non-charitable purpose trust for want of ascertainable beneficiaries.

questions.question: A pure non-charitable purpose trust with no anomalous exception and no identifiable beneficiaries is:

options.A: Void for uncertainty of subject matter
options.B: Void for breach of the beneficiary principle
options.C: Valid but held on resulting trust for the settlor
options.D: Valid if the trustee consents to perform the purpose

questions.explanation: A pure non-charitable purpose trust fails because it breaches the beneficiary principle established in Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804]: there is no identifiable beneficiary in whose favour the court can decree performance. It is void, not merely unenforceable. The property would result back to the settlor or their estate by way of resulting trust — but the trust itself fails due to the beneficiary principle, not uncertainty of subject matter.

questions.question: The rule against perpetuities applies to non-charitable purpose trusts. What is the practical consequence of this rule for anomalous exceptions such as Re Dean trusts?

options.A: They can last indefinitely if the trustee agrees
options.B: They must not last longer than the perpetuity period
options.C: They are void unless created by will
options.D: They are exempt from the rule against perpetuities as charitable trusts are

questions.explanation: Non-charitable purpose trusts, including anomalous exceptions, are subject to the rule against perpetuities. They must not last longer than the perpetuity period. This means that even where an anomalous exception is tolerated, it cannot run indefinitely. In Re Dean [1889], the trust was upheld for fifty years, which was within the perpetuity period.

questions.question: How does the Re Denley [1969] approach differ from the anomalous exception approach (e.g., Re Dean [1889])?

options.A: Re Denley trusts are enforceable by identifiable beneficiaries; anomalous exceptions are valid but unenforceable
options.B: Anomalous exceptions are charitable; Re Denley trusts are non-charitable
options.C: Re Denley trusts can last indefinitely; anomalous exceptions are time-limited
options.D: There is no practical difference — both categories are valid and enforceable

questions.explanation: The key distinction is enforceability. Trusts within the Re Denley [1969] principle are valid and enforceable by the identifiable individuals who benefit. Anomalous exceptions (Re Dean [1889], Pirbright v Salwey [1896]) are tolerated as valid but are unenforceable — they are trusts of imperfect obligation that rely on the trustee's willingness to perform.

questions.question: A settlor creates a trust 'for the construction and maintenance of a monument to my late husband in our local churchyard.' Which legal category best describes this trust?

options.A: A valid charitable trust as it serves a religious purpose
options.B: An anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle — a trust of imperfect obligation
options.C: A valid express trust for the benefit of the husband's estate
options.D: A void resulting trust

questions.explanation: Trusts for the erection and maintenance of graves and monuments are a recognised anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle, as illustrated by Pirbright v Salwey [1896]. They are valid but unenforceable — trusts of imperfect obligation that depend on the trustee's willingness to perform. They are not charitable trusts as they do not satisfy the public benefit requirement.

questions.question: In the context of non-charitable purpose trusts, what is the legal consequence if the trustee decides not to carry out the stated purpose in a trust of imperfect obligation?

options.A: The Attorney General may enforce the trust on behalf of the public
options.B: The court will appoint a new trustee willing to perform
options.C: No one has legal standing to compel performance and the trust may simply not be carried out
options.D: The beneficiaries may apply to court for specific performance

questions.explanation: In a trust of imperfect obligation, there is no beneficiary with standing to compel performance. If the trustee declines to carry out the purpose, no legal mechanism exists to force them to do so. This is the fundamental weakness of anomalous non-charitable purpose trusts and distinguishes them from the Re Denley [1969] category where identifiable beneficiaries can enforce the trust.

questions.question: A company creates a trust deed for 'the general benefit and welfare of our employees and their dependants.' Applying Re Denley [1969], this trust is most likely:

options.A: Void as a non-charitable purpose trust
options.B: Valid because the employees are identifiable individuals who can enforce it
options.C: Charitable as it promotes the relief of poverty among employees
options.D: Void unless the trust deed specifies the exact amount each employee will receive

questions.explanation: Applying Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969], where a trust is expressed as a purpose but benefits ascertainable individuals, those individuals can enforce it. Employees and their dependants are an identifiable class. The trust is therefore valid on the Re Denley principle — the beneficiary principle is satisfied because there are real, identifiable persons in whose favour the court could decree performance.

questions.question: Which of the following statements most accurately reflects the current law on non-charitable purpose trusts in Ireland?

options.A: All non-charitable purpose trusts are void without exception
options.B: Non-charitable purpose trusts are generally void, subject to a narrow category of anomalous exceptions and the Re Denley principle
options.C: Non-charitable purpose trusts are valid if registered with the Charities Regulator
options.D: The Charities Act 2009 abolished all categories of non-charitable purpose trust

questions.explanation: The current law is that non-charitable purpose trusts are generally void for breach of the beneficiary principle (Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804]). However, there are two ways a purpose trust may be valid: (1) narrow anomalous exceptions (animals, graves, private masses) which are trusts of imperfect obligation; and (2) the Re Denley [1969] principle where the purpose in substance benefits identifiable individuals.

questions.question: A testator leaves €10,000 'for the saying of private masses for my soul.' This trust is:

options.A: Void as a non-charitable purpose trust
options.B: Charitable as it advances religion
options.C: An anomalous exception — a valid trust of imperfect obligation
options.D: Valid only if the masses are to be said publicly

questions.explanation: Trusts for the saying of private masses are a recognised anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle. They are not charitable (private masses fail the public benefit test in charity law), but they have been tolerated by equity as trusts of imperfect obligation. The distinction between private and public masses is important: public masses may qualify as charitable (Re Caus [1934]), but private masses fall within this anomalous exception.

questions.question: What was the primary holding of Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804] in relation to non-charitable purpose trusts?

options.A: Purpose trusts for unincorporated associations are always valid
options.B: A trust for a purpose with no identifiable beneficiary is void because there is no one to enforce it
options.C: Non-charitable purpose trusts are valid only if limited to 21 years
options.D: Purpose trusts created by will are subject to different rules than inter vivos purpose trusts

questions.explanation: Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804] is the foundational case on the beneficiary principle. Grant MR held that a trust for 'such objects of benevolence and liberality' as the trustee might choose was void because there were no ascertainable beneficiaries to enforce it. The principle is that every trust must have a definite object and there must be somebody in whose favour the court can decree performance.

questions.question: A testator creates a trust 'for the upkeep of my cat, Whiskers, for as long as she shall live.' Which of the following best applies?

options.A: The trust is void as animals cannot hold equitable interests
options.B: The trust is charitable as it promotes animal welfare
options.C: The trust is an anomalous exception upheld by courts as a trust of imperfect obligation
options.D: The trust is valid under the Re Denley principle as the cat is an ascertainable individual

questions.explanation: Following Re Dean [1889], trusts for the maintenance of specific animals are recognised as an anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle. Such trusts are upheld as trusts of imperfect obligation — the trustee may carry out the purpose but cannot be compelled to do so. They are not charitable (a trust for one specific animal lacks public benefit) and they are not valid under Re Denley (animals are not persons with standing to enforce).

questions.question: Why is the distinction between a 'pure purpose trust' and a 'Re Denley-type purpose trust' significant for FE-1 examination purposes?

options.A: Only pure purpose trusts are subject to the Charities Act 2009
options.B: A Re Denley-type trust is enforceable by identifiable beneficiaries and therefore valid; a pure purpose trust fails the beneficiary principle unless it falls within anomalous exceptions
options.C: A pure purpose trust can last indefinitely while a Re Denley trust is limited to the life of current employees
options.D: There is no distinction — both fail for the same reason

questions.explanation: This distinction is central to purpose trust analysis. A pure purpose trust (with no beneficiaries at all) fails under the beneficiary principle from Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804] unless it falls within narrow anomalous exceptions. A Re Denley [1969]-type trust — expressed as a purpose but benefiting identifiable individuals — is saved because those individuals can enforce it. FE-1 candidates must identify which category applies in problem questions.

questions.question: In Re Dean [1889], the trust for the testator's horses and hounds was upheld for a period of how long?

options.A: The lifetime of the testator's widow
options.B: Twenty-one years from the date of the will
options.C: Fifty years
options.D: Indefinitely, as no perpetuity period was specified

questions.explanation: In Re Dean [1889], the trust for the maintenance of the testator's horses and hounds was upheld for a period of fifty years. This is within the common law perpetuity period (a life in being plus 21 years, or in some cases a fixed period). The case is the leading authority on trusts for specific animals as an anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would most clearly fall within the Re Denley [1969] principle rather than the anomalous exceptions approach?

options.A: A trust for the general care of stray animals in County Dublin
options.B: A trust for the saying of masses for deceased members of a religious order
options.C: A trust for the maintenance of a staff recreation facility for the employees of a named company
options.D: A trust for the maintenance of a war memorial in a public park

questions.explanation: Re Denley's Trust Deed [1969] involved a trust for the maintenance of a sports ground for the use of company employees. Goff J held this valid because the employees were identifiable individuals who could enforce the trust. Option C mirrors these facts exactly. Options A (stray animals generally) and D (war memorial) are pure purpose trusts. Option B falls within the private masses anomalous exception, not Re Denley.
